comment.body:
Hi Alma, thank you for the intelligent analysis.
I have a question: How can we tell which of the three greeks- speed, vanna, charm- has the greatest impact to the spot price movement?

Let's say we are in the middle of a "positive speed & positive vanna & negative charm" area, and the spot price is going upwards. Here is my understanding:
1. Positive speed has a downward effect because spot up→gamma up→MMs buy less or sell more delta
2. Positive vanna has an upward effect because spot up→vol down→delta down→MMs buy delta
3. Negative charm has an upward effect because time passes→delta down→MMs buy delta

But I'm not sure which one is dominating. I assume charm matters a lot right before the expiration, but not sure about other cases.

comment.body:
I would say it depends on the timeframe and the market conditions. This is how I view things:
Speed is essentially acceleration of gamma, so it is more of a momentum builder.
Vanna tends to be more powerful when the market conditions shift beyond expectations due to a reaction or when the market significantly underperforms in volatility compared to expectations, think of us climbing over a wall off worry or clearing an uncertainty. Also, not all deltas react the same to change in vanna.
Charm is about amplitude of positioning and the effect of theta decay gets stronger closer to expiry. It is also one of the weaker forces, so it often tends to be in charge when the others are absent aka low liquidity conditions. Think intraday when people are out to lunch and trading slows down.
Of course, pure sentiment can override all three in any which direction.
Don't ask me the math though, that is Alma's territory and what I am here for in the first place. It is a fun puzzle to solve though :)

comment.body:
Hi Alma, I do have some questions to properly decode your text :)
(1) You wrote "MMs have a bull put spread pattern" (or similar for butterfly). Do I interpret correctly that it means that the net gamma profile resembles the gamma profile of a typical bull put spread, so it can be very roughly approximated as if the MM is long put strike X + short put strike <larger than X> - resp. the customers' position is roughly the opposite (long put, below short put). Mapping it to the option strategy standards is more a mental model that allows to differentiate between gamma setups.

comment.body: You need to use separate chart window (free TV will not allow you) for each ticker otherwise it places the indicator with same parameters on all charts. This is why you can see charts compressed.
comment.raw_body: You need to use separate chart window (free TV will not allow you) for each ticker otherwise it places the indicator with same parameters on all charts. This is why you can see charts compressed.

comment.body:
Attention! The put spread is from dealers prospective. Retails are long skew (+Put OTM - Call OTM) viceversa, MMs (or dealers) are short skew (-Put OTM + Call OTM) -> so we are long gamma/vega on upside and short gamma/vega on downside.
--> Long Speed
comment.raw_body:

--> Long Speed

comment.body:
beta meassures the correlation.
Spot/vol beta means how much the spot and the volatility, atm vol, fix strike vol moves together.
when it is negative, they move opposite.
when it is positive, they move together.
of course, it has a spectrum, not black&white.

the "normal"  version is the negative (short) spot/vol beta, when spot up/vol down.
why? bcs it his case, investors are long stock, and use hedges to hedge the downside by buying OTM puts and selling OTM calls, buying the skew. (bcs skew is 25d put minus 25d call)
by doing this, they put market makers (who take the opposite of the trade: long OTM calls, short OTM puts, selling the skew) into long vanna: so as spot moves up, they become longer vega that they hedge by selling vol. 
This reflexively dumps volatility down if market moves up.
But if market moves down, MMs become shorter vega, means they have to buy vol. reflexively increasing volatility.
and not just that... gamma: they are longer upside gamma, and shorter downside gamma (positive speed). 
So, volatility compresses to the upside (as more trading against the price actions), and expands to the downside, resulting: NEGATIVE SPOT/VOL BETA.
 
but if the opposite happens, mania on the options market, and right-tail hedging: investors buying OTM calls and selling OTM puts, selling the skew. 
this puts market makers into long skew, and short vanna, means: as spot moves up, they become shorter vega, so they need to buy vega. 
Also shorter upside gamma, and longer downside gamma (negative speed)
So vol expands if market moves up, createing right-tail risk, low liquidity to the upside due to this one-sided crowded trade.
And compresses to the downside, as there where liquidity stands. 
This results POSITIVE SPOT/VOL BETA

if the bid effects the tails as well, tail OTM puts (that are synthetic VIX calls), bidding also vol of vol, you will see positive spot/vix beta. thats when blow-off top happens.
